coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots bridge NiO and Mn2O3 as highly efficient bifunctional oxygen electrocatalysts for rechargeable zinc-air batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Chenrui
Liao, Fan
Zhu, Wenxiang
Zhang, Yi
Ma, Mengjie
Yang, Junjun
Yin, Kui
Shao, Mingwang
Jiang, Binbin
coredata.dc:description: The development of high-efficiency, low-cost and durable bifunctional oxygen electrocatalysts is of great significance for the large-scale application of rechargeable zinc-air batteries. Herein, the carbon dots bridging nickel oxide and manganese trioxide through the oxygen-containing group of carbon dots (NiO-Mn2O3-CDs) for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is proposed. The optimal NiO-Mn2O3-CDs yields remarkable electrocatalytic activity with low overpotential (298 mV) to derive the 10 mA·cm−2 for OER and a high half wave potential (0.84 V vs. RHE) for ORR. The outstanding performances are attributed to the CDs covalently bridging Ni and Mn atoms, which reprogram the electronic structure of active sites and enhance charge transfer. Consequently, the rechargeable zinc-air battery test with NiO-Mn2O3-CDs as the air cathode deliver the excellent performances, including a high open voltage (1.51 V), a maximum power density (287 mW∙cm−2 @ 0.35 A∙cm−2) and robust cycling life, outperforming the commercial Pt/C + IrO2. This work presents an efficient strategy to construct the highly active and stability bifunctional air cathodes for electrochemical energy devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Manganese trioxide
Carbon dots
Oxygen evolution reaction
Oxygen reduction reaction
Rechargeable zinc-air batteries

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The project was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2017YFA0204800 ), National MCF Energy R&D Program ( 2018YFE0306105 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 51902003 , 51902217 ), Anhui Province Natural Science Foundation ( 2008085QB53 ), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology , the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), the 111 Project, Joint International Research Laboratory of Carbon-Based Functional Materials and Devices .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs and Mn2O3; (B) full XPS spectrum of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs; (C) C 1s spectrum of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs; (D) O 1s spectrum of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs and NiO-Mn2O3; (E) Mn 2p spectra of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs and NiO-Mn2O3; (F) Ni 2p spectra of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs and NiO-Mn2O3.
(A) TEM image of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs; (B) HRTEM image of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs and (C) HAADF-STEM image and its elements of Mn (red), O (green), Ni (blue) and C (yellow) with uniformly distribution.

(A) CV curves of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs in N2 and O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH with a scan rate of 0.1 V·s−1; (B) ORR polarization curves of Mn2O3, NiO-Mn2O3 and NiO-Mn2O3-CDs at 1600 rpm in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH; (C) K-L plots of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs electrodes at different potentials; (D) Tafel slopes of Mn2O3, NiO-Mn2O3 and NiO-Mn2O3-CDs; (E) The current-time (i-t) response for NiO-Mn2O3, NiO-Mn2O3-CDs and commercial Pt/C at 0.70 V vs. RHE in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH with 15 h test; (F) Chronoamperometric response of NiO-Mn2O3, NiO-Mn2O3-CDs and commercial Pt/C at 0.70 V vs. RHE in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH after introducing 2 mL 3 M methanol.

(A) The LSV curves of Mn2O3, NiO-Mn2O3 and NiO-Mn2O3-CDs in O2-saturated 1 M KOH without iR corrected; (B) EIS for Mn2O3, NiO-Mn2O3 and NiO-Mn2O3-CDs at the overpotential of 298 mV vs. RHE; (C) Cdl values of Mn2O3, NiO-Mn2O3 and NiO-Mn2O3-CDs at 1.15 V vs. RHE; (D) Half-wave potential E1/2 for ORR, OER potential Ej=10.

(A) V–i polarization curves of the charge and discharge process of NiO-Mn2O3, NiO-Mn2O3-CDs and Pt/C + IrO2; (B) the corresponding power density plots of NiO-Mn2O3, NiO-Mn2O3-CDs and Pt/C + IrO2; (C) The discharge-charge cycle properties of the zinc-air batteries assembled with NiO-Mn2O3-CDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots bridge NiO and Mn2O3 as highly efficient bifunctional oxygen electrocatalysts for rechargeable zinc-air batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Key Laboratory of Photoelectric-Magnetic Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anqing Normal University, Anqing 246011, PR China

School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices
Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the carbon dots bridges nickel oxide and manganese trioxide through the oxygen-containing group of carbon dots (NiO-Mn2O3-CDs) is successfully constructed and as an advanced bifunctional oxygen electrocatalysts. The CDs covalently bridging Ni and Mn atoms can reprogram the electronic structure of active sites and enhance charge transfer. Consequently, the optimal NiO-Mn2O3-CDs yields outstanding bifunctional activities for ORR and OER. Importantly, the rechargeable zinc-air battery test with NiO-Mn2O3-CDs as the air cathode delivers the excellent performances, outperforming the commercial Pt/C + IrO2.

The development of high-efficiency, low-cost and durable bifunctional oxygen electrocatalysts is of great significance for the large-scale application of rechargeable zinc-air batteries. Herein, the carbon dots bridging nickel oxide and manganese trioxide through the oxygen-containing group of carbon dots (NiO-Mn2O3-CDs) for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is proposed. The optimal NiO-Mn2O3-CDs yields remarkable electrocatalytic activity with low overpotential (298 mV) to derive the 10 mA·cm−2 for OER and a high half wave potential (0.84 V vs. RHE) for ORR. The outstanding performances are attributed to the CDs covalently bridging Ni and Mn atoms, which reprogram the electronic structure of active sites and enhance charge transfer. Consequently, the rechargeable zinc-air battery test with NiO-Mn2O3-CDs as the air cathode deliver the excellent performances, including a high open voltage (1.51 V), a maximum power density (287 mW∙cm−2 @ 0.35 A∙cm−2) and robust cycling life, outperforming the commercial Pt/C + IrO2. This work presents an efficient strategy to construct the highly active and stability bifunctional air cathodes for electrochemical energy devices.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing consumption of fossil energy and the serious environmental problems, advanced energy storage and conversion systems with high power density and low pollutant emission have been drawn much attention [1–3]. Rechargeable zinc-air batteries (ZABs) has been considered as a promising strategy to meet future energy demands due to the high theoretical energy density, rich storage resources, low cost, environment friendly and easy to recycle [4–6]. The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) play key roles in discharge and charge processes of rechargeable ZABs [7]. However, the sluggish kinetics of ORR and OER severely impede the extensive use of rechargeable ZABs [8–10]. Although the Pt and Ir based materials perform superior ORR and OER activity, their high costs and scarce resources hinder their widespread use in rechargeable ZABs [11–13]. Therefore, it is necessary to construct a high activity, low cost and environment friendly bifunctional catalyst toward ORR and OER.

Recently, Mn-based catalysts have been extensively studied due to the remarkable catalytic activity and abundant reserves [14,15]. In this respect, Ramírez and co-workers revealed that Mn2O3 exhibited high activity with 360 mV to achieve the 10 mA·cm−2 for OER in 1.0 M KOH [16]. Other works also confirmed that MnOx could significantly improve charge transfer and modulate the absorption energy of oxygen intermediates on the catalysts surface, resulting in the enhanced ORR activity [17]. The Wu and co-worker also manifested that nano-structured Mn2O3 exhibited the great potential for OER and ORR [18]. Unfortunately, owing to the relatively low conductivity of transition manganese oxides, the current density of most catalysts is quite low, which limits the development as bifunctional oxygen electrode catalysts. In addition, the experimental and theoretical studies have demonstrated that constructing heterostructure between nickel oxide and manganese oxides exhibited the promising potential to regulate the electrocatalytic performances [19–22]. For example, Chhetri et al. developed the NiO-doped Mn2O3 electrocatalyst supported on carbonaceous material for ORR. The optimal composite presented large current density (−0.314 mA cm−2) at 0.67 V vs. RHE in 1 M NaOH, which was higher than that of Mn2O3/C [21]. Luo et al. reported that the formation of NiO and MnO heterostructure was beneficial for OER [23]. Nevertheless, the electrocatalytic performance of above composite could not meet the commercial application.

Recently, the experimental date reveal that coupling carbon based materials with metal oxides can significantly improve their conductivity. Importantly, the carbon materials can alter the local electronic structure of active site, thereby reducing energy barrier of potential determining step for OER and ORR, which is beneficial for ZABs [24–27]. Carbon dots (CDs) has attracted much attention owing to its distinct physical and chemical properties, such as good chemical durability, low cost and rapid electron transfer characteristics. More important, the strong chemical interactions between metal oxide and CDs with abundant functional groups can efficiently reprogram the electrocatalytic activity and durability of composite [28,29]. Therefore, it is desirable to construct the metal oxide-CDs as the advanced bifunctional electrocatalysts for rechargeable ZABs.

In this work, we synthesize CDs covalently bridging NiO and Mn2O3 (NiO-Mn2O3-CDs) through a simple chemical reaction. Interestingly, the addition of CDs can effectively enhance the stability of composites and improve the electrocatalytic activity. Moreover, the optimal NiO-Mn2O3-CDs presents excellent bifunctional performance in terms with the high half-wave potential (0.84 V vs. RHE) for ORR and low overpotential (298 mV @ 10 mA·cm−2) for OER. As an air cathode for rechargeable ZABs, it provides an open circuit voltage of up to 1.51 V and a power density of 287 mW·cm−2 at 0.35 mA·cm−2.

Preparation of Mn2O3: Firstly, 1.78 g of manganese nitrate was dissolved in 350 mL of deionized water, and then 34.0 mL of 3% H2O2 solution and 1.0 mL ammonium were added with stirring for 6 h. subsequently, the resulting product was taken out and washed with ethanol and water for 3 times, respectively. Finally, after vacuum drying, the product was annealed at 500 °C for 3 h in the air atmosphere.

Preparation of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs: 10 mg Mn2O3 were dispersed in 10 mL 1.0 mM nickel chloride hexahydrate, and then 1 mL 1.0 mg·mL−1 CDs was added dropwise into above dispersion with continuous stirring for 6 h. Then, the above achieved product was washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol, and then was dried in the vacuum content. Finally, the product was annealed at 500 °C for 3 h in the air atmosphere. The synthesized catalysts with different contents of nickel chloride hexahydrate and CDs were listed in Table S1.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of Mn2O3 and NiO-Mn2O3-CDs are shown in Fig. 1
A, the peaks of 23.1, 32.9, 38.2, 45.2, 49.4, 55.2 and 65.8° correspond to the (211), (222), (400), (332), (431), (440) and (622) planes of Mn2O3 (JCPDS No. 41-1442), respectively, while the diffraction peaks of NiO and CDs for NiO-Mn2O3-CDs cannot be observed due to the low content. The Raman signals of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs loaded at 1350 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 are assigned to sp3 defects and the vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms ( Figure S1 ), which confirm the existence CDs in the as-prepared material [30]. By comparing the Raman spectra of NiO-Mn2O3 and Ni-Mn2O3-CDs in the Figure S2 , it is clearly observed that the peaks of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs exhibits an obvious blue shift against the NiO-Mn2O3, which confirms that the CDs can reprogram local structural deformation [31]. In addition, the Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs ( Figure S3 ) manifests that the surface has abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, such as OH (3425 cm−1), COOH (1422 cm−1), C=C (1570 cm−1).

X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were used to investigate the chemical state and electronic structure of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs. Fig. 1 B confirms that the constituent elements are mainly Mn, O, Ni and C (The peaks of Si corresponding to the substrates of silicon wafers). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s is deconvoluted into 284.7, 285.8 and 288.9 eV (Fig. 1 C), which correspond to C-C, C-O and C=O band, respectively [32]. Fig. 1 D reveals that O 1s spectrum of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs shifts to the positive binding energy with respect to NiO-Mn2O3, confirming oxygen-containing group of CDs covalently bridging Mn and Ni atoms [33,34]. The two peaks loaded at 529.6 and 531.5 eV in the O 1s spectrum of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs belong to Mn-O and Ni-O (Fig. 1 D), respectively [35]. While the other peak at 532.5 eV is assigned to C=O [36]. Interestingly, the Fig. 1 E clearly reveals that Mn 2p XPS spectrum of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs shifts to the low binding energy about 0.4 eV with respect to NiO-Mn2O3. Simultaneously, Mn 2p spectrum of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs can be fitting well with two peaks, the peaks loaded at binding energy of 653.4 eV and 641.7 eV are attributed to Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2. Fig. 1 F also manifests that the Ni 2p XPS spectrum of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs moves the negative binding energy about 0.4 eV against NiO-Mn2O3. The Ni 2p spectrum of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs could be divided into four peaks, which are attributed to Ni2+ (854.6 and 872.7 eV) and the satellite peaks (861.0 and 879.5 eV), respectively [37].The decreased binding energy of Ni 3p and Mn 3p is attributed to the oxygen-containing group of CDs, which strengthen the electron-withdrawing effect and further confirm CDs bridges NiO and Mn2O3 through oxygenic groups of CDs.

The morphology of as-prepared composite was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM). Fig. 2
A clearly reveals that NiO-Mn2O3-CDs displays the irregular and agglomerate sheet. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images at different magnifications also confirm the above results ( Figure S4 ). Compared with Mn2O3, the morphology of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs and NiO-Mn2O3 shows no significant change, indicating the CDs and NiO can not destroy the morphology ( Figure S5 ). The HRTEM image clearly exhibits that the Mn2O3, NiO and CDs close to each other (Fig. 2 B). Furthermore, there are three kinds of lattice fringes can be observed. Specially, the spacing of 0.24 nm and 0.21 nm correspond to NiO (111) and (200) planes. The intersection angle between (111) and (200) planes is 54.7°. The Mn2O3 can be verified via the lattice fringe of 0.27 nm for Mn2O3 (222) planes. Furthermore, the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm belongs to (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon can be also demonstrated by HRTEM. Fig. 3
C exhibits the high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs and corresponding elemental mapping, revealing distribution of Mn, O, Ni, and C elements.

The ORR performance of as-prepared electrocatalysts was appraised via the cyclic voltammogram (CV) in N2/O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH with rotating disk electrode (RDE). It is obvious seem that there is a significant reduction peak appears at 0.76 V vs. RHE for NiO-Mn2O3-CDs (Fig. 3 A) in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH. The reduction peak position of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs is higher than Mn2O3 (0.72 V vs. RHE) and NiO-Mn2O3 (0.75 V vs. RHE), indicating the NiO-Mn2O3-CDs exhibits great activity for ORR ( Figure S6 ). The LSV curve manifests that NiO-Mn2O3-CDs exhibits a half-wave potential of 0.84 V vs. RHE (Fig. 3 B), which is superior to those of Mn2O3 (0.76 V vs. RHE) and NiO-Mn2O3 (0.80 V vs. RHE). The results demonstrate that the CDs bridging NiO and Mn2O3 markedly enhance the ORR performance. Moreover, the LSV curves also confirm that the ORR activity is comparable with commercial Pt/C ( Figure S7 ). In order to achieve the optimal catalysts for ORR, the relationship between NiO and CDs content and performance was further investigated by LSV. Figure S8 manifests that the optimal NiO-Mn2O3-CDs exhibits the superior performances toward ORR. From the Fig. 3 D, NiO-Mn2O3-CDs displays smaller Tafel slope than these of Mn2O3 and NiO-Mn2O3, meaning the high ORR dynamic efficiency.

In addition, stability and methanol tolerant are crucial factors in the practical application. The durability of NiO-Mn2O3, NiO-Mn2O3-CDs and commercial Pt/C were tested at fixed potential of 0.70 V vs. RHE at 1600 rpm in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH. It can be seen from Fig. 3 E, the current attenuation rate of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs is about 17.0% after 15 h test. In contrast, NiO-Mn2O3 (loss about 30.0%) and commercial Pt/C (loss about 70.0%) exhibit a rapid current loss. In addition, the chronoamperometric technique was employed to evaluate the toxicity of methanol, and the attenuation rates of different catalysts are presented in Fig. 3 F. The current of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs drops slowly (4.6%), while the current of NiO-Mn2O3 and commercial Pt/C decrease about 6.5% and 40.8%, respectively. The results show that NiO-Mn2O3-CDs has a great potential application in methanol fuel cell.

OER activity of the electrocatalysts was tested by a three-electrode system with a scanning rate of 5 mV·s−1. To derive the current density of 10 mA·cm−2, the optimal NiO-Mn2O3-CDs only affords the overpotential of 298 mV (Fig. 4
A and S12), which is smaller than these of NiO-Mn2O3 (314 mV) and Mn2O3 (395 mV), confirming that the CDs is beneficial for improving the OER activity. The Tafel slope reflects the intrinsic property of the catalyst, which describes the determined steps in the OER reaction process [40]. The Tafel curves of electrocatalysts are converted through the corresponding LSV curves. As been seem in Figure S13 , NiO-Mn2O3-CDs exhibits the low Tafel slopes of 141.14 mV·dec−1 with respect to NiO-Mn2O3 (144.42 mV·dec−1) and Mn2O3 (160.09 mV·dec−1), demonstrating the fast OER kinetics reaction rate of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs. The remarkable performance is attributed to the oxygen-containing groups of CDs covalently bridging Ni and Mn atoms, which can accelerate the charge transfer and prompt exposing the active sites. In order to get insight the function of CDs bridging NiO and Mn2O3, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and electrochemical active area (ECSA) technologies were employed. The Nyquist plots exhibits that NiO-Mn2O3-CDs has the low charge transfer resistance (46.0 Ω) in comparison with NiO-Mn2O3 (76.6 Ω) and Mn2O3 (123.8 Ω) (Fig. 4 B and Table S2), suggesting its superior charge transport kinetics [41]. The ECSA of the electrocatalyst was characterized and calculated via the capacitance by the CV method at different sweep speeds ( Figures S14 and S15 ). The double layer capacitance (Cdl) of the catalyst is directly proportional to its corresponding electrochemically active area. The Cdl is calculated from the related linear equation, which obtained by linearly fitting the current density and sweep speed at 1.15 V vs. RHE. Fig. 4 C shows that the Cdl of the NiO-Mn2O3-CDs is 22.06 mF∙cm−2, which is larger than those of Mn2O3 (17.16 mF∙cm−2) and NiO-Mn2O3 (19.47 mF∙cm−2). This further explains the enhanced electrochemical performance of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs.

The stability of these electrocatalysts was measured by chronoamperometric response at 10 mA·cm−2. The current of NiO-Mn2O3-CDs maintains 71.5% compared with initial performance, revealing NiO-Mn2O3-CDs has superior stability against NiO-Mn2O3 (remained 8.9%) and Mn2O3 (remained 29.5%) after 55000 s test ( Figure S16 ). The result shows that the addition of CDs significantly enhanced stability due to oxygen-containing group bridges NiO and Mn2O3. In order to evaluate the application of the as-prepared composite in rechargeable ZABs, the potential difference between the OER potential at 10 mA·cm−2 and the ORR potential at E1/2 (ΔE = Ej=10-E1/2) is used to demonstrate their bifunctionality of an oxygen redox activity. Fig. 4 D and S17 show the overall polarization curve during ORR and OER. The ΔE of Mn2O3, NiO-Mn2O3, commercial Pt/C + IrO2 is 0.84 V, 0.71 V and 0.71 V, respectively. NiO-Mn2O3-CDs shows a minimum ΔE (0.69 V) among as-prepared samples, confirming the excellent electrocatalytic bifunctionality. Furthermore, this value also show an obvious advantage against some recently reported ORR and OER electrocatalysts (Table S3).

In general, oxygenic group of CDs bridging NiO and Mn2O3 was successfully constructed for ORR and OER by a two-step approaches. The optimal NiO-Mn2O3-CDs display excellent performance with a low overpotential (298 mV) for OER and a high half-wave potential (0.84 V vs. RHE) for ORR. The experimental results also confirm that CDs enhance the stability of the electrocatalyst. Notably, the NiO-Mn2O3-CDs as electrocatalysts in rechargeable ZABs performs a high open voltage of 1.51 V, a maximum power density of 287 mW·cm−2 at 0.35 A·cm−2 and superior durability. Therefore, the synthesized NiO-Mn2O3-CDs as cathode electrocatalyst exhibit great promising applications in rechargeable ZABs.

Chenrui Shao: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Fan Liao: Conceptualization, Methodology. Wenxiang Zhu: Investigation. Yi Zhang: Methodology. Mengjie Ma: Formal analysis. Junjun Yang: Validation. Kui Yin: Visualization. Mingwang Shao: Supervision. Binbin Jiang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0204800), National MCF Energy R&D Program (2018YFE0306105), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 51902003, 51902217), Anhui Province Natural Science Foundation (2008085QB53), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), the 111 Project, Joint International Research Laboratory of Carbon-Based Functional Materials and Devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.153642.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Regulated coordination environment of Ni single atom catalyst toward high-efficiency oxygen electrocatalysis for rechargeable zinc-air batteries
Energy Stor. Mater.

Chem. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zinc-iron bimetallic-nitrogen doped porous carbon microspheres as efficient oxygen reduction electrocatalyst for zinc-air batteries
Appl. Surf. Sci.

Zhong, ZIF-67 as continuous self-sacrifice template derived NiCo2O4/Co,N-CNTs nanocages as efficient bifunctional electrocatalysts for rechargeable Zn-Air batteries, ACS Sustainable. Chem. Eng. 6 (2018) 10021-10029.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: NiCo2O4 ultrathin nanosheets with oxygen vacancies as bifunctional electrocatalysts for Zn-air battery
Appl. Surf. Sci.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: B, N-doped defective carbon entangled Fe3C nanoparticles as the superior oxygen reduction electrocatalyst for Zn-air batteries
ACS Sustainable Chem. Eng.
